chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Adolescence: A Stage of Growth and Change

topics.topic_name: Stages of Life and the Onset of Adolescence

content.text: The journey of human life can be divided into distinct stages: infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood, and old age. Each person experiences these stages at their own pace, and the duration may vary from individual to individual.

Until around 10–12 years of age, most changes in the human body are related to height and weight. After this, other noticeable changes begin to occur, marking the onset of adolescence — a period of rapid growth and development typically occurring between the ages of 10 and 19 years.

content.title: Adolescence Defined
content.text: Adolescence is the stage of development between childhood and adulthood. It generally begins around 10 years of age and can continue up to the age of 19. During this period, the body prepares for adulthood.

Just as a seedling must grow and mature before it can produce seeds, humans also need to grow and reach maturity before they can reproduce. Adolescence is the period during which this maturity is achieved.

notes.section_title: 6.1 Growing With Age: The Teenage Years

content.text: Adolescence is characterised by several observable physical changes. These include changes in height, body structure, voice, hair growth, and skin. While most of these are visible, some changes occur internally and may not be immediately noticed.

content.title: Let Us Discuss — Changes from Grades 5 to 8
procedure: Take a jar and some paper slips.
Write down the changes you can notice among students as they go from Grades 5 to 8 — these could relate to height, strength, behaviour, or any other aspect.
Fold the slips and place them in the jar.
Mix all slips, then unfold them one by one and discuss the changes with the class.
content.observation: Common changes noticed include increase in height, weight gain, changes in voice, and appearance of facial/body hair.
content.conclusion: Multiple physical and behavioural changes occur during the transition from childhood to adolescence.

content.text: From birth, the human body undergoes continuous growth, including an increase in height. However, the rate of increase in height becomes more prominent and rapid during adolescence.

content.title: Key Observation
content.text: The timing and extent of height gain vary among individuals. These variations are completely normal — each person experiences growth at their own pace.

topics.topic_name: Changes in Body Structure, Weight, and Strength

content.text: During adolescence, boys typically grow taller, gain weight, their shoulders become broader, and their chests may get wider. Girls also undergo changes in height and weight, along with other body changes such as the development of breasts.

content.title: Body Changes in Boys vs Girls
items: Boys: Increase in height, broader shoulders, wider chest, weight gain.
Girls: Increase in height and weight, development of breasts.

content.text: In adolescent boys, the voice box (larynx) grows larger, causing the voice to sound hoarse or deeper. This growth of the voice box may appear as a visible bump in the throat region, called the Adam's apple. However, it is not noticeable in every individual.
The voice box also grows in adolescent girls, but it is not as large as in boys. This results in only a slight change in voice in girls.

content.title: Voice Box (Larynx)
content.text: The voice box is a structure in the throat that helps us to speak. Its enlargement in boys during adolescence leads to a noticeably deeper or hoarser voice.

topics.topic_name: Appearance of Hair in Different Parts of the Body

content.text: Both boys and girls experience the growth of hair in different parts of the body during adolescence, including the armpits and the pubic region.

content.title: Hair Growth Patterns
items: Both boys and girls: Hair growth in the armpits and pubic region.
Boys: Development of facial hair (moustache and beard in later stages), and possibly chest hair and back hair.
Girls: Primarily underarm and pubic hair growth.

content.title: Normal Variation
content.text: The timing and extent of hair growth vary among individuals. Some boys may have no significant facial or body hair, while others may have more. These variations are completely normal.

topics.topic_name: Changes in Facial Skin — Acne and Pimples

content.text: A common skin condition during adolescence is acne, in which small, reddish pimples appear — most commonly on the face. Acne occurs due to an increase in oily secretions from the skin during adolescence that can clog skin pores and lead to infections.

content.title: Acne vs Pimples
content.text: Acne is the skin condition; pimples are the physical manifestation of it. Increased oily secretions from the skin during adolescence are the root cause.

content.title: Tips to Manage Acne
items: Wash the face regularly with clean water.
Avoid touching the face with unwashed hands.
Maintain good personal hygiene.
Consult a doctor if acne is severe.

content.text: Certain changes during adolescence — such as change in voice, growth of facial and chest hair in boys, and development of breasts in girls — are not directly involved in reproduction. However, they help distinguish males from females. These are called secondary sexual characteristics.

content.title: Secondary Sexual Characteristics
content.text: Features that help distinguish males from females but are NOT directly involved in reproduction are called secondary sexual characteristics. They are natural signs that the body is preparing for adulthood.

content.title: Puberty
content.text: Puberty is the stage in which the body of an adolescent undergoes external and internal changes to develop into an adult capable of reproduction. Secondary sexual characteristics mark the onset of puberty.

content.title: Examples of Secondary Sexual Characteristics
items: Boys: Deeper voice, facial hair (moustache, beard), broader shoulders, Adam's apple.
Girls: Development of breasts, wider hips.
Both: Growth of hair in armpits and pubic region.

topics.topic_name: Internal Changes and the Menstrual Cycle

content.text: Adolescence is marked not only by observable changes but also by internal changes that are not visible from the outside. One such key change is the maturation of the organs involved in the process of reproduction.

An important internal change in adolescent girls is the onset of the menstrual cycle. The menstrual cycle recurs generally every 28–30 days and is commonly known as 'the period'. Many healthy girls may have longer or shorter cycles ranging from 21 to 35 days.

content.title: Menstrual Cycle
content.text: The menstrual cycle is a recurring natural process in girls/women that generally occurs every 28–30 days (range: 21–35 days). It is one of the important signs of good reproductive health.

content.title: Menstruation
content.text: The phase of the menstrual cycle when blood discharge occurs from the body is called menstruation. It may last for 3 to 7 days. Some girls may feel pain or discomfort in the lower abdomen during these days.
Menstruation begins at puberty. Usually, by the age of 45–55 years, menstruation stops naturally, marking the end of the reproductive capability in a woman's life.

Many wrong beliefs about menstruation lead to unnecessary fear, shame, or guilt. These beliefs have given rise to myths and taboos, including the physical isolation of menstruating girls — which is still unfortunately prevalent in some areas.

content.title: Scientific Outlook
content.text: Menstruation is a completely natural and healthy biological process. The myths and taboos surrounding it have no scientific basis. Encouraging a scientific outlook helps promote reproductive health and well-being of women.

content.title: Common Myths vs Facts
items: Myth: Menstruating girls should be isolated. Fact: Menstruation is natural; isolation has no scientific basis.
Myth: Menstruation is impure. Fact: It is a healthy sign of the body's reproductive system.
Myth: Girls should avoid physical activities during menstruation. Fact: Moderate physical activity is generally healthy and safe.

notes.section_title: 6.3 Emotional and Behavioural Changes in Adolescents

content.text: Adolescence is often marked by stronger and more complex emotions than childhood. These emotions can affect behaviour in both positive and challenging ways. Understanding how emotions influence behaviour can help adolescents make better choices and respond to situations thoughtfully.

content.title: Let Us List — Emotional and Behavioural Changes
procedure: Take a moment to think about any changes in your emotions or behaviour — or that of your classmates and friends — over the past one or two years.
List emotional changes in a table along with their probable effects on behaviour and ways for positive growth.
content.observation: Common emotional changes include mood swings, increased self-awareness, and stronger feelings of empathy or belonging.
content.conclusion: Adolescence involves significant emotional development alongside physical changes. Channelling these emotions positively is key.

rows: Fluctuation between high energy and low mood
Engage in varied activities like music, dance, or sports
Increased involvement in creative or new activities
Channel into compassion and social work

content.text: Emotional changes during adolescence can lead to behavioural changes such as: taking up social initiatives to support the needy, developing deeper interests in new areas, feeling attracted to peers, and mimicking the behaviour of others.

content.title: Key Point
content.text: Adolescence brings heightened curiosity and excitement. Understanding emotions helps adolescents make thoughtful decisions and avoid impulsive or harmful choices.

Since adolescence is a period of rapid growth and development, a nutritious and balanced diet is of utmost importance. The body requires proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins, and minerals in adequate amounts.

content.title: Let Us List — Food Sources and Nutrients
procedure: Based on locally available foods, identify healthy food sources.
Fill a table with the food sources, nutrients present in them, and how these nutrients help growth and development.
content.observation: Different food groups provide different essential nutrients required during adolescence.
content.conclusion: A balanced diet containing all food groups supports healthy growth and development during adolescence.

rows: Supports proper growth, strength, and energy
Helps in the formation of blood
Supports overall body functioning and immunity

content.title: Iron and Vitamin B12 Deficiency
content.text: Adolescents, especially girls, may suffer from blood-related health problems (such as anaemia) due to deficiency of iron or vitamin B12. Including iron-rich foods (spinach, pulses) and vitamin B12 sources (dairy, eggs, meat) in the diet is important. Vitamin B12 was studied by Dorothy Hodgkin, the third woman to win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1964).

Besides nutrition, maintaining personal hygiene is crucially important during adolescence. Regular cleanliness — particularly in the armpits and pubic region — helps protect against potential infections.

For girls, maintaining proper hygiene during menstruation is essential for both comfort and health. Menstrual hygiene can be maintained using sanitary pads, reusable cloth pads, or other appropriate products.

content.title: Disposal of Sanitary Pads
content.text: Used sanitary pads must be wrapped in newspaper and disposed of in a dustbin. Biodegradable sanitary pads are also available that are environment-friendly. Proper disposal ensures community health and environmental cleanliness.

content.title: Government Initiatives for Menstrual Hygiene
items: Menstrual Hygiene Scheme (MHS): Provides sanitary pads free or at reduced price to adolescent girls in rural areas; conducts awareness programmes.
Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram (RKSK): Improves overall adolescent health including menstrual health; encourages peer education.
Suvidha Sanitary Napkin Initiative: Provides biodegradable sanitary pads at affordable prices through Jan Aushadhi Kendras.
State-Level Initiatives: e.g., Shuchi Scheme (Karnataka), free sanitary napkin schemes in Tamil Nadu and Odisha.

content.text: Regular exercise and participation in games and sports are very important during adolescence. Physical activity keeps the body and mind fit and healthy, builds stamina, and boosts mood.

content.title: Benefits of Regular Physical Activity
items: Keeps the body physically fit and healthy.
Improves mental health and mood.
Builds strength and stamina.
Reduces stress and anxiety.
Encourages teamwork and social interaction.

content.text: Since adolescence is marked by new experiences, emotions, and peer influence, adolescents must be thoughtful and responsible while interacting with others — both in person and through social media.

content.title: Let Us Spread Awareness — Responsible Social Media Behaviour
procedure: Work in groups to design posters and pamphlets about responsible social media behaviour.
Paste them at designated places in the school.
Fill in a table of Dos and Don'ts for social media behaviour based on collective observations.
content.observation: Students identify key behaviours for safe and responsible online interaction.
content.conclusion: Awareness about responsible social media use helps create a safe digital environment for adolescents.

rows: Do not share personal photos with strangers or virtual friends
Do not spread rumours or false information
Do not share personal information with unknown people
Seek guidance from elders if in doubt
Do not engage with or respond to cyberbullying

content.title: Cyberbullying
content.text: Cyberbullying involves the use of digital devices (phones, computers, online platforms) to harass others by sending misleading messages, spreading false rumours, or sharing personal information without consent. If bullied online, seek help from parents and teachers immediately. Do not feel scared or helpless.

topics.topic_name: 6.4.5 Avoiding Harmful Substances — Learn to Say NO

content.text: Adolescents may face pressure from peers to consume harmful substances such as tobacco, gutka, cigarettes, beedi, alcohol, or illegal drugs. Since adolescence is marked by curiosity and excitement, it is important to be aware of the dangers and stay firm in saying 'NO'.

content.title: Substance Abuse
content.text: Substance abuse refers to the regular and uncontrolled use of harmful substances. These substances are not only harmful to physical and mental health but are also addictive — once people start, they develop a strong and persistent urge to use them again and again.

content.title: Health Effects of Harmful Substances
items: Tobacco/Beedi/Cigarette: Damage to lungs, breathing difficulties, increased risk of cancer.
Alcohol: Liver damage, memory loss, impaired judgement.
Illegal drugs: Severe damage to brain and organs, life-threatening conditions.
All substances: Risk of addiction, mental health issues, decline in academic and social performance.

content.title: How to Overcome Addiction
content.text: The first step to overcoming addiction is seeking help and support from family and friends, and talking to trusted people such as parents or teachers. Counselling and medical advice are also helpful. The National De-addiction Helpline number is 14446 (Government of India).

content.title: Nasha Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan
content.text: Launched by the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment, Government of India, this campaign aims to spread awareness about avoiding substance abuse through active participation of youth, women, and the community. Special focus is on prevention of substance abuse among early-age children.

notes.section_title: 6.5 The 'Why' Question for Adolescence — Role of Hormones

topics.topic_name: Hormones and Their Role in Adolescence

content.text: Many changes in adolescence — including menstruation, growth in height, voice change, and other signs of puberty — are mainly caused by hormones. Hormones are certain chemicals produced in our bodies that play a crucial role in regulating various aspects of growth and development.

content.title: Hormones
content.text: Hormones are chemical messengers produced in different parts of the body. They are released at appropriate times in response to signals from the brain. Hormones regulate growth, development, and various body functions. Some hormones also influence mood and behaviour.

content.title: Key Facts About Hormones
items: Hormones are chemicals produced in the body.
They are released in response to signals from the brain.
They regulate growth, development, and reproduction.
Some hormones influence mood and behaviour.
They are produced in different parts of the body (glands).
Both physical changes (height, voice, breasts) and internal changes (menstrual cycle) are controlled by hormones.

glossary.definition: The period of development between childhood and adulthood, generally from age 10 to 19 years, marked by rapid physical, emotional, and behavioural changes.
The stage in which the body of an adolescent undergoes external and internal changes to develop into an adult capable of reproduction.
Features that help distinguish males from females but are not directly involved in reproduction, such as growth of facial hair in boys and development of breasts in girls.
A recurring natural process in females that generally occurs every 28–30 days (range 21–35 days), indicating reproductive health.
The phase of the menstrual cycle during which blood discharge occurs from the body, lasting 3 to 7 days.
A visible bump in the throat region of boys caused by the growth of the voice box (larynx) during adolescence.
A skin condition characterised by small, reddish pimples, commonly appearing on the face during adolescence due to increased oily secretions clogging skin pores.
Chemical messengers produced in different parts of the body that regulate growth, development, reproduction, and some aspects of behaviour.
The regular and uncontrolled consumption of harmful addictive substances such as tobacco, alcohol, or illegal drugs.
The use of digital devices or online platforms to harass others by sending misleading messages, spreading false rumours, or sharing personal information without consent.

summary_points: Adolescence is the period of change from childhood to adulthood, generally beginning around age 10 and lasting until age 19.
Physical changes include increase in height, weight gain, voice change (Adam's apple in boys), hair growth in armpits and pubic region, and appearance of acne.
Secondary sexual characteristics distinguish males from females but are not directly involved in reproduction; they mark the onset of puberty.
In girls, adolescence is marked by the onset of the menstrual cycle, a recurring natural process every 28–30 days; menstruation is the phase involving blood discharge lasting 3–7 days.
Menstruation is a natural biological process; myths and taboos surrounding it have no scientific basis.
Adolescence also brings emotional and behavioural changes including mood swings, self-exploration, stronger emotions, and peer influence.
A balanced diet, good personal hygiene, regular physical activity, and responsible social behaviour are essential for a healthy adolescence.
Harmful substances such as tobacco, alcohol, and drugs are addictive and damaging; adolescents must firmly say 'NO'.
Most changes during adolescence are regulated by hormones — chemicals produced in the body in response to signals from the brain.
